memoryAids.content: DOT: Don't Overcomplicate Things. A dot product results in a scalar (a single number). CROSS: Create Right-handed Orthogonal System. A cross product results in a vector perpendicular to the original two.
memoryAids.helps_remember: Distinguishes between the scalar result of a dot product and the vector result of a cross product.

memoryAids.content: Think of the dot product like a work report. The amount of work done (the scalar result) depends on how much force (first vector) is applied in the direction of the movement (second vector). Think of the cross product like a torque wrench. The output torque (resulting vector) is perpendicular to both the force you apply (first vector) and the wrench itself (second vector).
memoryAids.helps_remember: Relates abstract operations to tangible physical concepts of work and torque.

memoryAids.content: For vector addition/subtraction: Visualize walking a path. Adding vectors is like walking one leg of the trip and then the next. The resultant vector is the straight line from your start to your finish. For the cross product magnitude: Visualize the area of the parallelogram formed by the two vectors. The length of the base is |a|, the height is |b|sinθ, so the area is |a||b|sinθ.
memoryAids.helps_remember: Provides a spatial, geometric interpretation of vector addition and the magnitude of the cross product.

memoryAids.content: For a zero dot product: a dot b = 0. Remember 0 looks like a circle, which is symmetric. This helps remember that a zero dot product means the vectors are orthogonal (perpendicular), a symmetric relationship.
memoryAids.helps_remember: Links the numerical result of zero to the geometric property of perpendicularity.

memoryAids.content: If the cross product makes you fret, just remember i, j, k, and do not forget: i cross j is positive k, that is the right-hand rule, okay?
memoryAids.helps_remember: Uses a simple rhyme to recall the right-hand rule and the result of crossing standard basis vectors in R3.

memoryAids.content: For the distributive property a • (b + c) = a•b + a•c: Imagine projecting the sum of vectors b and c onto a. The total projection length should equal the sum of projecting b and c onto a separately.
memoryAids.helps_remember: Uses the concept of projection to justify why the dot product distributes over addition.
